title: Persistent high humidity and disease risk
summary: The greenhouse shows persistent high relative humidity and low VPD during production conditions, increasing disease risk and requiring an operational decision.
suspected_issue: Persistent high humidity with insufficient climate correction and rising disease-risk exposure.
symptoms: Relative humidity remains abnormally high in Sector B during the daytime.
VPD is believed to be too low for healthy production conditions.
Ventilation response appears insufficient for the observed moisture load.
The crop is in production phase and disease exposure is becoming a concern.

observed_signals.interpretation: Humidity is above the expected range and supports a disease-risk concern.
Temperature is not extreme, so humidity control is the main concern rather than heat stress.
Radiation is sufficient for daytime climate correction measures to matter.
Outdoor wind conditions do not suggest that ventilation should be fully blocked.

operational_context.note: The issue is affecting Sector B during normal daytime production conditions.
operational_context.impact: This is not an isolated maintenance test or a night-only anomaly.

operational_context.note: No explicit backup climate-control path is available for the affected sector.
operational_context.impact: The service should not rely on backup switching as the main response.

agronomic_context.note: Tomato crop is in production phase with disease sensitivity under persistent moisture stress.
agronomic_context.impact: A delayed response may affect yield quality and increase phytosanitary risk.

business_context.note: Sector B is part of normal production flow and quality stability matters.
business_context.impact: The case should be treated as operationally important even if it is not yet a harvest emergency.

notes: This case is designed to broaden policy coverage beyond irrigation backup scenarios and test how the service behaves under climate-risk conditions without a clear fallback asset path.
